final_answer:
FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9
  PREDICTED_SMILES: CCOC#CS(=O)(=O)c1ccccc1
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C10H10O3S with 6 DBE, 1H NMR shows monosubstituted benzene (5H) and ethyl group (-OCH2CH3), 13C NMR shows 8 signals including alkyne carbons at 79.88/90.66 ppm and no carbonyls, consistent with phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ethyl ether. HRMS confirms molecular formula.

steps.content:
You are a senior expert in organic synthesis and spectroscopic structure elucidation, working in a laboratory to analyze an unknown small organic molecule with ID 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9. You may call tools to obtain MS, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and other data, and then infer its structure.
[Your Objectives] 1. Use all available spectroscopic and molecular information to elucidate the structure. 2. When information is insufficient, plan and call only the necessary tools to collect evidence. 3. Provide the most plausible candidate structure (as SMILES) and a confidence score. 4. Efficiency matters (important): each tool call simulates a real experiment with time and monetary cost.
   Prioritize correctness of the final structure, but minimize the number of tool calls by reasoning deeply
   from the data already available.

[Available Tools] The tool list below is injected at runtime and always matches the tools you can actually call:
- Measure_MW(uuid): Get molecular weight. Returns a float in Da.
- Measure_Formula(uuid): Get molecular formula. Returns a string, e.g. "C22H32N2O3S".
- Get_13C_NMR(uuid): Get 13C NMR peak list/description text. Returns a string, e.g. "13C NMR (75 MHz, CDCl3): δ ...".
- Calculate_DBE(uuid): Calculate degrees of unsaturation (DBE). Requires sample SMILES and RDKit. Returns a float; returns -1.0 if unavailable (unknown).
- Get_1H_NMR(uuid): Get 1H NMR peak list/description text. Returns a string, e.g. "1H NMR (300 MHz, CDCl3): δ ...".
- Check_Data(uuid): List which experimental data are available for the sample. Returns a list of tool names, e.g. ["Measure_MW", "Get_13C_NMR", "Get_IR"].
- Get_IR(uuid): Get IR spectrum description text. Commonly used to confirm functional groups (e.g., C=O, O-H).
- Get_19F_NMR(uuid): Get 19F NMR description text. Typically meaningful only when the molecule contains F.
- Get_31P_NMR(uuid): Get 31P NMR description text. Typically meaningful only when the molecule contains P.
- Get_HRMS(uuid): Get HRMS description text/peak info. Commonly used to confirm molecular formula, isotopic pattern, or exact mass.
- Get_MS(uuid): Get MS (non-HRMS) description text/peak info. Commonly used to estimate molecular weight and inspect fragment peaks.
- Get_Melting_Point(uuid): Get melting point information. Returns a string (typically includes unit and range, e.g. "mp 120–122 °C").
- Get_TLC(uuid): Get TLC information (Rf, eluent/solvent system, etc.). Returns a string.
- Get_Optical_Rotation(uuid): Get optical rotation information (e.g., [α]D value, solvent, concentration). Returns a string.
[System Behavior Note (important)] - To avoid invalid calls: if you do not call any tool at the very beginning, the system may automatically
  run Check_Data once to confirm which data are available for the current sample. Unless you truly need to
  verify availability, you do not have to treat Check_Data as a mandatory first step.

[Suggested Reasoning Workflow (adjust as needed)] 1. Strategy planning: identify the most critical uncertainty first; avoid blindly requesting all spectra.
   If you are unsure whether certain data exist or want to avoid wasted calls, you may call Check_Data.
2. Iterative data acquisition (important): acquire data step-by-step. After each tool result, pause to
   reason and decide whether you are still uncertain. Only then request the next most informative test.
   Prefer calling at most ONE new tool per iteration unless multiple calls are strictly necessary.
3. Basic data: call Measure_MW and Measure_Formula to obtain molecular weight and molecular formula. 4. First pass: use Calculate_DBE to estimate unsaturation and form initial hypotheses. 5. Core elucidation: prioritize Get_1H_NMR (and Get_13C_NMR only if needed). Use chemical shifts,
   integrals, splitting patterns, and carbon environments to build one or more plausible scaffolds.
6. Targeted validation: only when ambiguity remains, cautiously call Get_IR / Get_HRMS (etc.) to obtain
   specific discriminating evidence.
7. Once you have the most plausible candidate structure, provide its SMILES. 8. If the evidence is still clearly insufficient for a unique structure, explicitly state what is uncertain,
   and provide your best current candidate with an appropriate confidence.

[Output Requirements (very important)] When you believe you are done and no further tool calls are needed, your final reply must include the following structured block (key names must match exactly). You may provide natural-language reasoning before it, but the block below is mandatory:
FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9
  PREDICTED_SMILES: <SMILES of your best candidate; if you cannot give one, write "UNKNOWN">
  CONFIDENCE: <a decimal between 0 and 1, e.g., 0.8>
  REASON_BRIEF: <1–3 sentences summarizing the key evidence supporting your choice>

Notes: - If multiple candidates are plausible, you must output only your top choice in PREDICTED_SMILES;
  you may mention other candidates in the free-text reasoning.
- If you believe the data are insufficient for a reliable structure, you must set PREDICTED_SMILES="UNKNOWN"
  and explain why in REASON_BRIEF.

The unique identifier of the current sample is 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9. Start by planning which tools to call, then proceed step by step to complete the structure elucidation.
I'll start by checking what experimental data is available for this sample to plan my analysis efficiently.
Great! I have access to molecular weight, molecular formula, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and HRMS data. I'll start by getting the molecular formula and molecular weight to establish the basic framework.
Now I'll get the molecular weight to confirm this formula and then calculate the degrees of unsaturation.

The molecular formula C10H10O3S has a calculated molecular weight of 210.25, which matches the measured value. Now I'll calculate the degrees of unsaturation (DBE) to understand the level of unsaturation in the molecule.

For C10H10O3S:
- DBE = (2C + 2 - H - X + N)/2 = (2×10 + 2 - 10 - 0 + 0)/2 = (22 - 10)/2 = 6

This suggests 6 degrees of unsaturation, which could indicate aromatic rings (4 DBE for benzene) plus additional unsaturation or rings.

Now I'll get the 1H NMR data to understand the proton environment.

The 1H NMR shows:
- Aromatic protons: 7.58-7.64 (m, 2H), 7.55 (tt, 1H), 7.41-7.47 (m, 2H) - total of 5 aromatic protons
- Quartet at 4.48 ppm (2H, J = 7.1 Hz)
- Triplet at 1.51 ppm (3H, J = 7.1 Hz)

This suggests a monosubstituted benzene ring (5 aromatic protons) and an ethyl group (-CH2CH3) with the quartet-triplet pattern. The chemical shift of the quartet at 4.48 ppm is quite downfield for a typical alkyl chain, suggesting it's attached to an electron-withdrawing group.

Now I'll get the 13C NMR data to understand the carbon framework.

The 13C NMR shows 8 distinct carbon signals, which is consistent with the molecular formula C10H10O3S. Let me analyze this:

- Aromatic carbons: 132.92, 131.90, 128.89, 117.32 (4 signals, but we expect 6 aromatic carbons for a monosubstituted benzene)
- Unusual signals: 90.66 and 79.88 ppm - these are in the range typical for alkynes or sp carbons
- 69.51 ppm - this could be the CH2 group attached to an electron-withdrawing group
- 14.61 ppm - the methyl group of the ethyl

Wait, I notice something important: the 13C NMR shows only 8 signals, but C10H10O3S should have 10 carbons. This suggests either:
1. Some carbons are equivalent (symmetry)
2. The aromatic region might have overlapping signals

But more importantly, those signals at 90.66 and 79.88 ppm are very characteristic of a terminal alkyne! Terminal alkynes show C≡C-H carbons around 70-90 ppm, with the terminal carbon (C-H) around 70-80 ppm and the internal carbon around 80-90 ppm.

Let me reconsider the structure:
- Molecular formula: C10H10O3S
- DBE = 6
- Monosubstituted benzene (4 DBE, 5H, 6C)
- Terminal alkyne (2 DBE, 2C, 1H) - but wait, terminal alkyne would have 1H, but our 1H NMR only shows 5 aromatic + 2 + 3 = 10 protons total, which matches C10H10O3S
- Ethyl group (-OCH2CH3, 2C, 5H)

But that would be: benzene (C6H5-) + alkyne (-C≡CH) + ethyl (-OCH2CH3) = C9H8O, which doesn't match.

Let me think differently. The formula C10H10O3S with 6 DBE:
- Benzene ring: 4 DBE
- Remaining 2 DBE could be from carbonyl groups or other unsaturation

The 13C signals at 90.66 and 79.88 are unusual for typical carbonyls (which appear 160-220 ppm). These look more like acetylenic carbons.

Actually, let me check if this could be phenyl propiolate or similar. But we have sulfur in the formula.

Another possibility: this could be a phenylsulfonyl compound with an alkyne.

Let me consider: phenylsulfonyl acetylene would be C6H5SO2C≡CH, which is C8H6O2S (too small).

What about ethyl phenylsulfonylpropiolate? C6H5SO2C≡CCO2Et?

That would be: C6H5 (C6H5) + SO2 + C≡C + CO2Et (C3H5O2) = C10H10O4S, which has one extra oxygen.

Wait, let me recalculate: C6H5SO2C≡CCO2Et
- C: 6 + 2 + 3 = 11 carbons (too many)

Alternative: phenylsulfonyl propionate with unsaturation?

Let me think about the 13C signals again. The absence of carbonyl signals (160-220 ppm) is notable. All signals are below 133 ppm, which suggests no carbonyl groups.

This is puzzling. C10H10O3S with no carbonyls? The oxygens must be in other functional groups.

The 1H NMR shows the ethyl group with CH2 at 4.48 ppm, which is typical for -OCH2- (ethyl ester would be ~4.3 ppm, but ester would show carbonyl in 13C).

But we don't see carbonyl carbons in 13C NMR. The highest chemical shift is 132.92 ppm, which is aromatic.

This suggests the oxygens might be in sulfoxide/sulfone groups or ethers.

For a sulfone group (SO2), the 13C NMR wouldn't show additional carbons beyond the aromatic ones.

Let me consider phenyl vinyl sulfone derivatives, but that wouldn't explain the alkyne-like signals.

Wait, those signals at 90.66 and 79.88 - could they be from a different functional group?

Another possibility: these could be from a disubstituted alkyne, but terminal alkynes have the C-H proton around 2-3 ppm, which we don't see in the 1H NMR.

Looking back at the 1H NMR: it only shows aromatic protons (7.4-7.6 ppm) and the ethyl group (4.48 and 1.51 ppm). There's no signal around 2-3 ppm for a terminal alkyne proton.

This means the alkyne must be internal, not terminal. An internal alkyne would have no alkyne proton, which fits.

So: C10H10O3S
- Benzene ring: C6H5- (5H, 6C)
- Internal alkyne: -C≡C- (2C, 0H)  
- Ethyl group: -CH2CH3 (5H, 2C)
- Total so far: C10H10, need O3S

The ethyl group's CH2 at 4.48 ppm suggests it's attached to oxygen: -OCH2CH3

So we have C6H5-C≡C-OCH2CH3 plus SO2? But that would be C10H10O3S if it's C6H5-SO2-C≡C-OCH2CH3

Let's check: phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ethyl ether = C6H5SO2C≡COCH2CH3
- C: 6 + 2 + 2 = 10 ✓
- H: 5 + 5 = 10 ✓  
- O: 2 (from SO2) + 1 (from ether) = 3 ✓
- S: 1 ✓

Perfect! This matches the molecular formula.

Now check the NMR:
- 1H NMR: 5 aromatic protons (monosubstituted benzene), -OCH2- quartet at 4.48 ppm, -CH3 triplet at 1.51 ppm ✓
- 13C NMR: 
  - Aromatic carbons: 4 signals (some may overlap in monosubstituted benzene)
  - Alkyne carbons: ~79-90 ppm range ✓
  - -OCH2- at 69.51 ppm ✓
  - -CH3 at 14.61 ppm ✓

The DBE: benzene (4) + alkyne (2) = 6 ✓

This structure makes sense: ethyl (phenylsulfonyl)ethynyl ether or phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ethyl ether.

The systematic name would be ethyl 2-phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ether, but more accurately: ethyl (phenylsulfonyl)ethynyl ether.

The SMILES would be: CCOC#CS(=O)(=O)c1ccccc1

Let me verify this structure accounts for all data:
- Molecular formula C10H10O3S ✓
- 1H NMR: aromatic 5H, OCH2 (2H, q), CH3 (3H, t) ✓
- 13C NMR: 8 signals expected (benzene has 4 types due to symmetry, alkyne 2C, OCH2, CH3) ✓
- Chemical shifts: alkyne carbons 79-90 ppm, OCH2 at 69.5 ppm (reasonable for ether attached to electron-withdrawing sulfonyl) ✓

This seems to be the correct structure.

The HRMS data shows [M+NH4]+ at m/z 228.0689, which matches the calculated value for C10H14NO3S. This confirms our molecular formula C10H10O3S (since M+NH4 = M + 18, so M = 210, which matches our earlier MW measurement).

All data consistently support the structure: ethyl (phenylsulfonyl)ethynyl ether, with SMILES CCOC#CS(=O)(=O)c1ccccc1.

FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9
  PREDICTED_SMILES: CCOC#CS(=O)(=O)c1ccccc1
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C10H10O3S with 6 DBE, 1H NMR shows monosubstituted benzene (5H) and ethyl group (-OCH2CH3), 13C NMR shows 8 signals including alkyne carbons at 79.88/90.66 ppm and no carbonyls, consistent with phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ethyl ether. HRMS confirms molecular formula.

FINAL_RESULT:
  UUID: 20c387d2-3bb0-4dee-a4be-2a12961007a9
  PREDICTED_SMILES: CCOC#CS(=O)(=O)c1ccccc1
  CONFIDENCE: 0.95
  REASON_BRIEF: Molecular formula C10H10O3S with 6 DBE, 1H NMR shows monosubstituted benzene (5H) and ethyl group (-OCH2CH3), 13C NMR shows 8 signals including alkyne carbons at 79.88/90.66 ppm and no carbonyls, consistent with phenylsulfonyl ethynyl ethyl ether. HRMS confirms molecular formula.
